English translation of source text:
It is believed that the solstices have been celebrated at Stonehenge for thousands of years. The start of the longest day of the year in that part of the planet brought together about 10,000 people at the Neolithic monument, according to Wiltshire Police, the county in the UK where it is located. "There was a lovely and friendly atmosphere, the sun was shining, and the dawn was welcomed with shouts of joy," said Kate Logan from the English Heritage organization. Many who came to greet the solstice at Stonehenge believe that the Neolithic structure is a very spiritual place. Some visitors take advantage of the solstice at Stonehenge to reflect or recharge. The stones of the circle align with the sunrise on the summer solstice and with the sunset on the winter solstice. On the summer solstice, the sun rises behind the Heel Stone, the ancient entrance to the site, and the rays of sunlight are channeled into the center of the structure. The hill known as Glastonbury Tor in Somerset, in southern England, also attracted crowds wanting to celebrate the first day of summer. Many modern "druids" (who follow ancient Celtic religious beliefs) also go to await the summer solstice at Stonehenge.

English translation of candidate summary:
The summer solstice is celebrated at Stonehenge, in southern England.

But why do cats not like to cooperate with each other or with humans? Or conversely, why do many other animals, whether domestic or wild, prefer to live in groups? The advantages of living in a group are quite common in nature. Birds, antelopes, and fish live in groups. Predators often hunt in groups as well. Lions, which belong to the cat family, also live in groups. Being in a group means being safer. A predator will only hunt one of them, and if there are a hundred animals in the group, the chance of being hunted drops to one percent. When alone, that chance is one hundred percent. Some animals live in groups for their benefits. It means that the animals in the group have many eyes. The likelihood of spotting an approaching predator increases, allowing for a better chance to escape. This cooperation in vigilance also allows individual animals to spend more time finding food. Sài Gòn Giải Phóng online nói văn bản của Cục Báo chí ngày 04/06/2008 kết luận trang mạng vietbao.vn "đã và đang cung cấp thông tin lên mạng Internet khi chưa được cơ quan có thẩm quyền cho phép." Các báo nói rằng một trong số những lý do khiến vietbao.vn bị "sờ gáy" là họ đã lấy tin tức từ các báo khác mà không xin phép. Một lý do nữa quan trọng không kém, theo báo Lao Động online, là trang web này đã "đăng lại một số bài viết từ các blog về các vấn đề nhạy cảm, phức tạp, không phù hợp với chủ trương và chính sách đối ngoại của Nhà nước Việt Nam." Hiện Việt Nam chưa có quy định cụ thể nào về việc quản lý blog, dẫu cho chủ đề này đã được đưa ra thảo luận từ hồi 08/2007. Trong một cuộc phỏng vấn với BBC hồi cuối năm ngoái, Cục trưởng Cục Báo chí Hoàng Hữu Lượng nói viết blog không phải là điều cấm kỵ. Tuy nhiên, Chánh Thanh tra Bộ Văn hóa Thông tin Vũ Xuân Thành nói chính quyền có thể dựa vào Nghị định 55/CP (về quản lý, cung cấp và sử dụng dịch vụ Internet) để xử lý hành vi phát tán nội dung, hình ảnh "đi ngược thuần phòng mỹ tục và thông tin chống Đảng, chống Nhà nước" thông qua blog. Được biết, trong năm 2007 và 2008, Cục Báo chí đã từng gửi văn bản tới Thanh tra Bộ, cơ quan công an và trung tâm internet Việt Nam, yêu cầu các cơ quan hữu quan kiểm tra hoạt...
